title: The Tamil Nadu Government Stumbling Along a Path of Decline!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy; the frozen source chapter remains unchanged and authoritative. The page images restore the OCR-omitted opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,` and confirm the economic figures and English newspaper quotations. The stray Latin fragment `GOMI` is visibly printed in the source after the Central Statistics Office quotation; it is retained as a source anomaly rather than silently rewritten. Clearly mechanical OCR errors and word breaks are repaired. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In the local-body by-elections being held on 18 September 2014, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, DMDK, PMK, MDMK, Manithaneya Makkal Katchi, Puthiya Tamilagam, Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi, Indian Union Muslim League, Congress and other parties have boycotted the contest. They said they could not participate in the face of the ruling party’s lawlessness and wrongdoing against democratic norms.

Although only the BJP and Communist parties contested these by-elections, elections were due in 3,075 places. In 1,589 of them, the State Election Commission and the AIADMK acted together either to reject nominations or make candidates withdraw, and ruling-party candidates were declared elected unopposed. To that extent, democracy is flying its triumphant flag under AIADMK rule in Tamil Nadu!

In this farce, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa first said she could see no opponents in the field. Then, like a heroine appearing in comedy scenes, she announced that she was going to campaign. At enormous expense, a new helipad was built for her at Thoothukudi. She travelled there and to Coimbatore, read out exactly the campaign notes written for her, handed the same text to the newspapers, and returned.

Reading her speech in Coimbatore, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said, “Tamil Nadu is travelling on the path of growth,” and supplied figures given to her by officials. She began her reply by saying: “Political-party leaders here have cited the Central Statistics Office’s data for 2012-2013 and alleged that Tamil Nadu’s growth has declined. It is my duty to explain the true position to you.” I looked to see what statistical evidence she would offer to demolish that argument.
In my Questions and Answers column on 14 September 2014, I had said:

“Ordinarily, there is a substantial difference between economic-growth figures issued by State Directorates of Statistics and those supplied by the Central Statistics Office. Figures released by State Directorates are published only after receiving the State Government’s approval. They therefore tend to favour the State Government and cannot be regarded as entirely reliable.

“The Central Statistics Office, however, obtains no one’s approval. Even the Prime Minister cannot intervene in its work. No one therefore doubts its neutrality or reliability. *The Hindu* published on 10 September 2014 the Central Statistics Office’s report on the economic growth of the States. Its very first paragraph begins: ‘Bihar is the fastest growing State while Tamil Nadu is the worst performer, the latest data released by the Central Statistics Office has revealed.’

“According to the Central Statistics Office’s latest data for 2012-2013, Bihar was the fastest-growing State and Tamil Nadu the worst-performing State, at the very bottom. The Office’s data on national GDP growth and State-wise growth show that the national average was 4.5 per cent. Bihar achieved growth of 10.73 per cent and secured first place in the country.

“Almost every State grew at or above the national average of 4.5 per cent. Tamil Nadu occupies the last place in the comparative ranking of State growth. Its growth rate is only 3.9 per cent, far below the national average. In the ranking of eighteen States released by the Central Statistics Office, the great ‘honour’ of finishing last has gone to Tamil Nadu under Chief Minister Jayalalithaa.”

Leaders of other parties in Tamil Nadu also cited these figures and condemned the AIADMK government responsible for this dreadful condition. It was to them that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa replied at her Coimbatore election meeting. Do you know what her answer was?

She said: “Tamil Nadu experienced a severe drought in 2012-2013. Because both the southwest and northeast monsoons failed, and because Karnataka refused to release the water due to us, Tamil Nadu recorded negative growth in agricultural production.” In other words, she did not say that the opposition parties’ figures were wrong. She merely cited the severe drought that year and sought an adjournment.

Jayalalithaa further said in Coimbatore that the position had changed completely in 2013-2014 and that the Central statistical organisation had estimated higher Gross State Domestic Product growth. But the newspapers reported only that the organisation had released total-output growth figures for 2012-2013.

While answering on total growth, why did Chief Minister Jayalalithaa not answer the industrial-growth figures published by *The Times of India*, which I had also cited? Does she accept that all those figures are true?

Industrial investors had said Tamil Nadu was not a suitable State in which to operate industries; that factories had suffered from continuous power cuts over the past three years; and that senior decision-makers, including the Chief Minister, were not easily accessible. They said Tamil Nadu’s overall growth in 2012-2013 was a meagre 4.14 per cent and that its industrial growth had declined year after year.

Manufacturing growth in Tamil Nadu stood at 29.18 per cent in 2009-2010 under the DMK government. It fell to minus 1.31 per cent in 2012-2013 under Jayalalithaa. Industrial growth stood at 20.93 per cent in 2009-2010 under the DMK government. It declined to 5.38 per cent in 2012-2013 and fell still further to 1.61 per cent in 2013-2014. Why was the Chief Minister unable to answer any of this at the Coimbatore meeting?

The World Economic Forum’s *Competitiveness of Indian Cities* report recently said: “Chennai is not the right place to start an industry. Ahmedabad ranks first. But the position of Chennai was different four years ago. According to the report published in 2010, Chennai had overtaken Bengaluru in the rankings.” What is the Chief Minister’s answer to that?
The Chief Minister also spoke about electricity in Coimbatore. Yet ten days have passed since an industrialist told *The Times of India*:

“Nothing can be done without electricity. Industries in Tamil Nadu have to purchase power from outside to keep operating. Most power companies in Tamil Nadu, particularly wind-energy companies, sell electricity to the Government and then wait for months to be paid. At a time when the Tamil Nadu Government must undertake several action plans, it is doing nothing. Windmills will not generate after September. We are going to wait until October to see what can possibly be done.”
What is her answer?

In another article in the same newspaper, under the headline “NAMMA CHENNAI, NOT A GLOBAL CITY,” it was reported that Ford, Michelin and Hyundai had established their Indian headquarters in Chennai during the DMK administration. But because of adverse measures under the present AIADMK government, all three automobile companies had moved their headquarters to other States. Where is Chief Minister Jayalalithaa’s answer to that?
When the DMK left office, paragraph 5 of the Budget presented to the Assembly in 2011 stated:

“Because of the sound economic-growth programmes implemented in Tamil Nadu, the State’s economy is in a strong position. At constant prices, growth in 2006-2007 was 12.64 per cent in agriculture, 12.91 per cent in industry and 16.33 per cent in services, while the State’s total output grew by 14.85 per cent. Per-capita income, which was Rs. 39,692 in 2005-2006, rose to Rs. 69,377 in 2009-2010.”
But this year’s Budget, presented under AIADMK rule on 13 February 2014, said:

“Even as the State Government strives hard to achieve equitable growth, economic growth and investment have been affected for a second consecutive year because the Union Government failed to stimulate growth and improve the weakening macroeconomic environment.”
It merely blamed the Union Government. It supplied no information showing that Tamil Nadu’s Gross State Domestic Product growth had risen.

Jayalalithaa’s proclamation that she would make Tamil Nadu the foremost State has become an empty slogan. The Vision Plan for Tamil Nadu has become a mere paper plan. Tamil Nadu’s growth has run aground on every front.

In these circumstances, Jayalalithaa’s election claim that “Tamil Nadu is travelling on the path of growth,” made to divert the voters, has no meaning whatever. In truth, Tamil Nadu is not travelling on a path of growth. It is stumbling along a path of decline that only deepens the people’s distress!
With affection,
M.K.
18 September 2014
